speaker: interviewer
text: With Eva Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and he couldn't make it already.

speaker: interviewee
text: If he can't make it, he can't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number six.

speaker: interviewee
text: With him, what are you going to do? So he'll see this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Weiss, now, was there anything that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I like to say is that I'm sometimes think back, that all the things, what happened, I try not to be bitter. And I tried not to relate it to my children that was growing up. I was a tough mother. I wanted my kids to be just perfect. [LAUGHS] And I worked-- we both worked very hard for nothing but education. And I felt if they could have the education which I was deprived, and I couldn't have it, nobody will ever take it away from them. And we think that we give them a future in life, no matter where life is going to throw them or what going to be. And I hope that they are ever going to listen to the story, as much as we gave today. And they will be stronger by it. And-- and they will be able to face life, whatever it comes, in their way. And I hope my grandchildren will see it one day, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know my oldest one--

speaker: interviewee
text: They will.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Erika ?] is very interested. She's very warm and emotional, and to me, especially. And she wants to know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something you'd like to say to them right now? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, when did they read-- see the story, and they see me, as-- as they know me, and if they could just visualize what, the little girl, I went through, and I hope they can comprehend it because I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there anything that any of you would like to say to your mother and to your wife? ZOLTAN (HUSBAND): Nothing, no. Just hang in there. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that we're proud of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're very proud of her. She's a very strong woman. And she's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she thinks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes you are. ZOLTAN (HUSBAND): [? Well, no you are. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: She is.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that we're here doing this-- ZOLTAN (HUSBAND): Yeah. --I think shows that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her grandchildren are very proud of her. And she's left a beautiful legacy for her children and her grandchildren by doing this special project, and by telling her story to the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. ZOLTAN (HUSBAND): That's right. OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Weiss, who is this in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother when she was engaged to be married to my father. And I think, back of that picture, is 1919. And I took this away from my aunt, because that's all I have of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karolina, Karolina [? Kohn. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Debrecen, in-- in her hometown. That's where she lived. ZOLTAN (HUSBAND): Hungary. Yeah. It's Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, also when he was engaged to be married. And my aunt evidently, you know, got the picture from him in-- in Hungary. Yeah, they were still in Hungary. Which it's taken in 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Hermaan Lefkovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh-- OK, Mrs. Weiss, and who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister. The oldest child was Edith. And in here, she's sending this picture to the United States, in 1939, to my cousins and to my aunt. And it's been done in Olaszliszka, in a garden. It's not a great shot. But I'm thankful that I have that, so the face comes back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Yeah. OK, and Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another picture of my sister. She mailed that from Olaszliszka to here, to United State, in 1940. And she looks, already, sad in there. I think things were already rough around our home. I could see it. Because her sadness is written on her face.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how old is she here?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in '21. So she's 19 years old in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] OK, and Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me in-- going to Sweden, which is in 1945, in July-- June, no July. July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is like a passport picture, going to-- to Sweden from Belsen, actually. And this is in-- in-- in '45, July, which was after liberation. And we already have, you know, our hair growing back. And we have our clothes on, like a blouse and skirt or something there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the closest. I can't remember what I look like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Weiss, what is this a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is in Sweden in Loka Brunn. Which I told you that it's a beautiful summer resort. And I have no idea. I think my cousin, [? Magda, ?] who was a dressmaker, she tore a stoled a drapery fabric from one of the rooms, and made a man-made bathing suit for me. Because it's already July. And when I started to mingle between the public, I went-- the first thing, we went to swim in a lake. Matter of fact, I almost drowned in the lake, thinking that I can cross it. [LAUGHS] And I don't know the rest of the girls' name, except me in there and a-- a Swedish young man, who was our [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with us. And he pulled me out of that lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- you know where? Second-- you know, I can't see without my glasses. The second or-- second one in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the-- to the left. The third or the second to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're right in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, right in the middle. OK? [BACKGROUND NOISE] OK. Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my cousin, Magda, who now is-- lives in Caracas. And we are the two survivors of five cousins. And we're together, in Sweden. And this is already in 1946, like, January. And by that time, we gain weight. We have normal Western clothes on. We bought it from our money, which came from the United States, from the relatives. So we at least look normal there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do remember exactly where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Loka Brunn, right where we spend-- I was there about five months or six months. And then we were transferred to a school. To-- you know, because I was under 18. So I had to learn Swedish, German, and English. Not German, just Swedish and English. That's all they taught us. So this is where we are together. Then it snow, and then-- and we learning how to ski. They taught us how to ski and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life was beautiful in there for us. It was a lift.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Bela, who now lives in Pontiac, in here, in Detroit area. And this is the time when he went to Israel from Hungary. He was-- he evidently was in a concentration camp, the last transport from Budapest. He was caught. But he went in-- in a labor camp, slave labor camp, and then I think escaped. He went through an awful lot in tortures, which, of course, left a lot of mark on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This has to be taken about '47, as soon as he arrives to Israel, '47 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, I think the back said 1951. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Or '51? Might be. Well, he was-- he was an Israeli soldier and everything. So I don't know when the first transport got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, might be '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Mrs. Weiss, who is this couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is us, my husband and I. This is at our honeymoon, in Chicago. And it's in 1947, November 10. ZOLTAN (HUSBAND): Hey, you're sitting [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Very, very nice. OK, and Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, this is a picture I have from my brother, [? Laszlo. ?] [? Latzi, ?] he was called. And his wife, at the end of the table. In front of them is my aunt, my mother's sister, who I live with-- whom I lived with in this state for over a year or so. Till I got married. And other end is my husband's family and us. And this was taken at [INAUDIBLE], in my oldest son's bar mitzvah, which is-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year was it? Let's see. He's 47. 30-- it's 30-- 32 years ago exactly, in January. It's going to be 33 years in January. OK. And which one is your brother in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother came to this country in 1956. I brought the whole family out in Hungarian Revolution and died in here, in leukemia, 10 years after. So he's deceased.

speaker: interviewer
text: But which one is he in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the mustache?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with the mustache, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end with my sister-in-law, on the side of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Weiss, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is our family. My husband's 70th birthday, our children give him a beautiful luncheon. And I think this is the time where we're, you know, really together, the grandchildren. My daughter there, the two sons, and the kids. [? Erika, ?] who gave a beautiful speech to Daddy that time, to Papa. And [? Danny ?] and [? Aaron, ?] [? Laurie, ?] [? Emily, ?] and [? Mark. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where is the picture of the son that we didn't meet? Is he on the outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken at our [? lovely ?] country club.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but and-- but which-- which son did we not meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest son, on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Jeffrey ?] Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and Mrs. Weiss, who is this group of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: This group is our grandchildren. I start with [? Danny ?] Weiss, and [? Erika ?] [? Walters, ?] [? Emily ?] [? Walters, ?] [? Aaron ?] [? Weiss, ?] [? Lauren ?] [? Weiss, ?] and [? Marky ?] [? Weiss. ?] And this is, again, at the 70th birthday of my husband. And I think my son, who delivered a beautiful speech standing there. My oldest son, who couldn't be with us here today. And the family was together. The [? went ?] [? down ?] about four years ago, it's going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's about 1991.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. OK, and Mrs. Weiss, what is this that we're looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Lefkovits family, the genealogy my nephew's keeping from our background, our grandfather, and their parents, and all. But the one is highlighted is my father-- my grandfather, my grandmother, then my father, my mother, and us children. My father had-- you know, they had six children, two girls and four boys. Then uncles and aunts, and their family. And here, uncle and aunts and their family, and another family. These are all uncles and cousins. We all went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so what is highlighted?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the immediate relatives who went to Auschwitz with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --all of the names that are highlighted here, all went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Olaszliszka. And from [INAUDIBLE], we had ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's excellent.
